Conversation-1.content: I've been thinking about building a platform that lets people upload conversations from different AI assistants and continue them elsewhere without losing context. Initially it's just a side project, but I want the architecture to scale if it grows.
That's an interesting problem. The biggest challenge isn't importing conversations—it's preserving context across different assistant formats. Are you aiming for exact replay or semantic continuity?
Semantic continuity. Different assistants format conversations differently anyway. I care more about preserving meaning than preserving every token.

Then I'd separate the project into three layers: ingestion, normalization, and retrieval. The ingestion layer parses each platform, normalization converts everything into a common schema, and retrieval reconstructs context for future conversations.
Makes sense. I was thinking of MongoDB because conversations are basically documents.

MongoDB is a reasonable starting point if conversations remain hierarchical. If you later need analytical queries across millions of messages, PostgreSQL may become attractive, but MongoDB keeps iteration fast.
Let's start with MongoDB then. I don't want to overengineer.
Good. Keep the schema flexible since different providers expose different metadata.
Tech stack: Next.js frontend, FastAPI backend. Python will also make ML integration easier later.
That's a solid combination. FastAPI also handles async workloads well, which helps if imports become long-running.
Authentication... maybe Clerk?
Clerk integrates nicely with Next.js. Unless you need self-hosting, it's a good choice.
Hosted is fine.
Then Clerk should reduce a lot of boilerplate.
One more thing. I only have a MacBook Air M5 with 16GB RAM, so I don't want anything that requires huge local models.
That's useful to know. Prefer API-based models for generation and lightweight embedding models if you compute embeddings locally.
Actually embeddings could probably be generated in the background.
Yes. Imports can finish first, then queue embedding generation separately.
I don't want users waiting five minutes before they can open their imported chat.
Exactly. Separate ingestion from indexing.
Speaking of indexing, should I use Pinecone?
Since you're just starting, I'd avoid managed vector databases. MongoDB Atlas Vector Search or FAISS locally are cheaper options.
Atlas Vector Search sounds interesting since we'd already have MongoDB.
Agreed. Fewer moving parts initially.
I also want users to import ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini conversations eventually.
Then define an internal normalized format immediately. Every importer should convert into that representation instead of exposing provider-specific fields.
Something like Conversation, Message, Attachment?
Yes. I'd also include references, timestamps, tool invocations, and arbitrary metadata.
Why arbitrary metadata?
Future providers will expose fields you don't anticipate. A flexible metadata object prevents schema migrations for every importer.
That's fair.
Keep required fields minimal and everything else extensible.
Should attachments live in the same document?
Probably not. Store metadata in MongoDB but files in object storage.
S3-compatible storage. MinIO locally and AWS S3 in production would keep environments similar.
I forgot—I don't actually have AWS credits.
Then MinIO locally is perfectly fine for development.
Cool.
You'll also avoid accidental cloud costs while experimenting.
I'm trying to avoid spending more than about $25/month during development.
That's realistic if you avoid managed AI services for everything except generation.
Speaking of AI, eventually I want compression algorithms to summarize older conversations.
That's an important architectural decision. I'd avoid overwriting the original conversation. Store summaries as derived artifacts.
So original plus compressed versions?
Exactly. Think of compression outputs as cacheable layers, not replacements.
Nice. That also makes benchmarking easier.
Right. You can compare compressed context against the original conversation.
The benchmark is actually one of the reasons I'm building this.
Are you planning to evaluate multiple compression algorithms?
Yes. I'd like users to plug in different approaches.
Then define an algorithm interface early. Every implementation should accept the same normalized conversation object.
Maybe something like compress(conversation, budget)?
That's a clean API. Budget could represent tokens, characters, or another resource constraint depending on the implementation.
Good point.
I'd make budget explicitly specify units to avoid ambiguity.
Like {"value": 4000, "unit": "tokens"}?
Exactly.
Should I expose the algorithms through REST endpoints?
Yes. Keep compression asynchronous if conversations become large.
So POST /compress starts a job?
And GET /jobs/{id} returns progress.
Makes sense.
You'll appreciate that later when processing large imports.
Random question—I saw someone recommending Celery.
Celery is mature but introduces Redis and worker management. If your workload is modest, FastAPI background tasks or Dramatiq could be simpler.
I'd rather avoid Redis if possible.
Then don't introduce it until there's a clear need.
Okay, no Redis for now.
That decision also keeps deployment simpler.
While we're on deployment, I'm thinking Railway.
Railway works well for early-stage projects.
Better than Render?
Railway generally provides a smoother developer experience, though Render is also capable. Your choice mainly depends on pricing and deployment limits.
I'll start with Railway.
Good. Avoid premature infrastructure complexity.
I guess Docker is still worth using.
Absolutely. Containerizing early reduces environment inconsistencies.
I'll probably have frontend and backend in separate containers.
That's a common setup. Add MinIO as another service in docker-compose.
Should MongoDB also be inside Compose?
Yes. That gives new contributors a one-command setup.
I should probably write a README while things are still simple.
Future you will appreciate that.
Before I forget, I want the entire system to be open source eventually.
Then pay attention to licensing from the beginning, especially for imported data formats and any third-party models.
Good point.
Also avoid tightly coupling to proprietary APIs if you expect community contributions.
Speaking of models, I was considering Qwen.
Qwen has strong instruction-following performance, but abstract the model provider so switching later doesn't require application changes.
So something like an LLMProvider interface?
Exactly. Compression algorithms shouldn't care whether responses come from Qwen, GPT, Claude, or a local model.
Nice.
That abstraction will likely pay off much sooner than expected.

Conversation-2.content: I started sketching the project structure last night.
Great. What does it look like so far?
Something like: * apps/web * apps/api * packages/shared * packages/sdk
I like that. A monorepo makes sense if both the frontend and backend evolve together.
Yes. Turborepo works well with Next.js and shared packages. It also keeps builds efficient as the project grows.
I wasn't sure whether the Python backend fits nicely in a Turborepo.
It does. You won't get build caching for Python in quite the same way, but the repository organization still helps.
I'd keep Python dependency management independent though—Poetry or uv instead of trying to force everything through npm.
I've been hearing a lot about uv recently.
It's very fast and becoming popular. Unless you already have a strong preference for Poetry, uv is a reasonable choice.
I also realized conversations can contain images, PDFs and even code files.
That's why separating attachment storage from message storage was a good decision earlier.
Your normalized schema should let a message reference multiple attachments.
I initially assumed one attachment per message.
That assumption will probably break. Users often upload several files before asking a single question.
Depends. If you want to preserve conversation branching from assistants like ChatGPT, yes.
It's easy to overlook initially because most chats look linear.
Maybe children shouldn't be stored directly?
Correct. Parent references are enough. Child relationships can be computed.
I started implementing the importer interface.
Parsing converts input into your internal model. Validation ensures required fields exist and timestamps are sane.
It also makes debugging much easier.
Speaking of debugging, I hit my first issue.
Some exported conversations don't have timestamps.
Then timestamps should probably be optional in your schema.
But retrieval ordering depends on them.
You can fall back to message order when timestamps are unavailable.
Record that ordering was inferred so downstream components know.
Provenance metadata becomes surprisingly useful later.
I also want to measure whether compression preserves important facts.
You'll need an evaluation pipeline, not just the compression algorithms.
That's fine. Just avoid designing yourself into a corner.
My Git history is already messy.
During feature development, no. Before merging to main, yes.
Descriptive commit messages will help more than perfect history.
I currently have: POST /import GET /conversation/{id} POST /compress
I'd also expose a conversation list endpoint.
Cursor pagination scales better than offsets.
MongoDB can handle basic full-text search, but semantic retrieval should use embeddings separately.
Remember we decided those are generated asynchronously after import.
I accidentally started generating embeddings during upload.
That explains why imports felt sluggish.
I'll move them into a background worker.
Although now I need a queue.
You could initially persist pending jobs in MongoDB and have a polling worker.
Slightly, but acceptable for an MVP.
I was about to introduce RabbitMQ.
I also started writing the compression interface.
Did you settle on the method signature?
We discussed making budget include explicit units.
That avoids confusion between tokens and characters.
The SDK should probably expose that too.
Speaking of the SDK, should it be Python or TypeScript?
Which users are you targeting first?
I almost started with TypeScript because the frontend is Next.js.
That's a common instinct, but your benchmark users are different from your frontend users.
You can generate an OpenAPI client for TypeScript later.
Their pricing gets expensive if this becomes popular.
Auth.js gives more control but requires managing more infrastructure.
Unless pricing is already blocking you, I'd postpone changing authentication.
I found a package that directly parses ChatGPT exports.
Does it also handle Claude and Gemini?
Then I'd avoid depending on it.
It creates inconsistent parsing behavior across providers.
A unified importer is worth the extra effort.
That's probably our first rejected approach then.
I also experimented with storing raw provider JSON only.
So we'll always store both raw exports and normalized conversations.
Yes. Raw data for traceability, normalized data for application logic.
Storage is cheaper than debugging missing information later.
Especially if future normalization bugs require reprocessing historical imports.
Re-importing without asking users to upload everything again is a huge operational advantage.
Those kinds of decisions often don't seem important until months later.

Conversation-3.content: I started thinking about the normalization schema in more detail.
Any changes from the original idea?
Slightly. I now have Conversation, Message, Attachment and Participant.
Adding Participant is useful. It avoids duplicating author information on every message.
Some conversations contain system prompts, tools and function outputs.
I'd treat them as a separate message type instead.
So participant remains the author while messageType distinguishes normal text, tool invocation and tool result?
Speaking of rendering, I don't actually want to perfectly recreate ChatGPT's UI.
That's probably wise. Focus on readability rather than pixel-perfect replication.
Users care more about continuity than identical styling.
Add horizontal scrolling rather than trying to wrap every cell.
Normalization slows it down to about 700.
Earlier you mentioned only having a MacBook Air M5 with 16GB RAM. Background generation was definitely the right decision.
Yeah, interactive imports became unusable otherwise.
Good thing we caught that before integrating everything.
I almost switched to OpenAI embeddings.
If someone imports hundreds of conversations, the bill adds up.
Especially if you're subsidizing free users.
Maybe expose multiple embedding providers later.
Keep the embedding interface separate from the LLM interface.
Some users might even skip embeddings entirely.
Especially if they only want archival imports.
I also started defining the compression interface more carefully.
Instead of returning plain text, it now returns structured summaries.
Summary text, extracted facts, open questions and confidence.
Downstream systems can decide whether to trust the summary or retrieve original messages.
Especially when aggressive compression drops details.
I also thought about including citations.
It'll let users jump back to supporting evidence.
And it'll help benchmark factual preservation.
Structured outputs will probably outperform plain summaries in evaluations.
Just embedding the whole conversation and retrieving chunks.
Retrieval quality was okay but token usage exploded.
Yes. Retrieval alone doesn't solve long-context efficiency.
I started drafting the benchmark format too.
Original conversation, compressed version and evaluation prompts.
You'll eventually want reference outputs or scoring becomes inconsistent.
That's possible, but keep human evaluation in mind.
Is uvicorn listed as a dependency or only installed locally?
Check your project dependencies rather than your global environment.
It often works locally because the package is already installed.
Yep. It's only in my development environment.
Are you using a multi-stage build?
Especially this early. Reproducibility becomes harder as dependencies grow.
Have you set one up yet?
Import correctness affects every downstream component—normalization, indexing, compression and benchmarking.
A subtle parsing bug can silently corrupt everything else.
I guess importer coverage should be close to 100%.
I'll start collecting sample exports from different providers.
Just remember to anonymize them before committing.
Synthetic examples are fine too, but real-world edge cases tend to expose issues much faster.
Import pipelines always encounter stranger data than expected.
